general_information.title: Linear Regression: Modeling with Straight Lines
general_information.description: Comprehensive guide to finding the equation of a line that models data using linear regression in Desmos. Covers both exact line determination (2 points) and best-fit trend lines (3+ points) for SAT Math data analysis problems. Includes strategies for slope-intercept, point-slope, and standard forms.
general_information.sat_topic: Algebra: Linear Equations and Data Analysis
prerequisites: Understanding of linear functions and slope
Ability to read coordinate points from graphs or tables

linear_regression_fundamentals.slope_intercept_form: y = mx + b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept.
linear_regression_fundamentals.slope_interpretation: The rate of change. For every 1-unit increase in x, y changes by m units.
linear_regression_fundamentals.y_intercept_interpretation: The initial value. The value of y when x = 0.
linear_regression_fundamentals.correlation: R² indicates fit quality. R² = 1 means perfect linear relationship. R² close to 1 indicates strong linear trend.

exact_vs_best_fit.exact_2_points: When given exactly 2 points, the regression line passes exactly through both points.
exact_vs_best_fit.best_fit_3_plus: With 3+ points, the line is an approximation that minimizes the total error across all points.

table_method.description: Enter data points in a table and run regression using the tilde operator.
steps: Create table with x₁ and y₁ columns
Read m and b from output

point_slope_method.description: Find two points and use slope formula, then write the equation.
point_slope_method.application: Best when only 2 points are provided.

list_method.description: Use Desmos lists for quick data entry without a table.
list_method.note: This is faster than table entry for many SAT problems.

slider_method.description: Use sliders to manually find the best-fit line.
slider_method.note: Adjust sliders until the line appears to fit the data. Less accurate than regression.

auto_regression.description: Use Desmos' auto regression feature after entering data.
steps: Enter table with x₁ and y₁
Desmos automatically selects the best-fit model
auto_regression.note: Quickest for data analysis problems on SAT.

required_desmos_actions.description: Create a table with x and y columns to organize coordinate data.
required_desmos_actions.note: Table headers must match exactly for regression to work.

required_desmos_actions.description: Run linear regression using tilde notation to find slope and intercept.
required_desmos_actions.note: Always use ~ (tilde), not =. The tilde tells Desmos to find the best-fit parameters.

required_desmos_actions.description: For exactly 2 points, find the equation by direct computation.
required_desmos_actions.syntax: y₁ ~ m x₁ + b (same as regression for exactly 2 points)
required_desmos_actions.note: With exactly 2 points, regression gives the exact line.

required_desmos_actions.description: Use lists instead of tables for faster data entry.
required_desmos_actions.syntax: x = [values], y = [values], y ~ m x + b
required_desmos_actions.note: Lists are faster to type for 3+ points.

required_desmos_actions.description: Desmos automatically displays R² when running regression.
required_desmos_actions.interpretation: R² = 1 means perfect linear fit. R² > 0.9 is strong. R² < 0.5 is weak.

expected_desmos_outputs.description: Regression parameters m (slope) and b (y-intercept) appear in a gray box.
expected_desmos_outputs.interpretation: These define the linear equation y = mx + b. Use these values directly.

expected_desmos_outputs.description: Coefficient of determination (R²) appears with the regression output.
expected_desmos_outputs.interpretation: Measures how well the line fits the data. R² close to 1 means data points lie very close to the line.
expected_desmos_outputs.example: R² = 0.98 (excellent fit), R² = 0.85 (good fit), R² = 0.45 (poor fit)

expected_desmos_outputs.description: A line passing through or near the data points appears on the graph.
expected_desmos_outputs.interpretation: Visual confirmation that the line captures the data trend. Points should be close to the line.
expected_desmos_outputs.example: The line should follow the general direction of the scatter plot points.

expected_desmos_outputs.description: Hover or click on the line to see coordinates.
expected_desmos_outputs.interpretation: Confirms the line passes through expected points or shows predicted values.
expected_desmos_outputs.example: At x = 5, the line shows y = 15.5

interpretation_and_answer_logic.instructions: The regression output provides the slope m and y-intercept b. Substitute these into y = mx + b to write the final equation. Check that the line visually aligns with the data trend. For SAT questions, identify whether they ask for the slope (rate of change), y-intercept (initial value), a specific prediction (evaluate at given x), or the equation itself.
interpretation_and_answer_logic.format: y = [m]x + [b] (slope-intercept form) or equivalent form based on question requirements.

sat_strategies.equation_question: The SAT asks for the equation. Write y = mx + b with computed m and b.
sat_strategies.prediction_question: The SAT asks for a value at a specific x. Enter f(x) = y = mx + b, then evaluate at the given x.
sat_strategies.interpretation_question: The SAT asks for the meaning of slope or intercept. Slope is the rate of change (per unit). Intercept is the initial value (at x = 0).
sat_strategies.trend_question: The SAT asks about trend. Positive slope → increasing. Negative slope → decreasing.

coaching_steps.title: Enter the data points into Desmos
coaching_steps.instruction: Click the '+' button, select 'Table', and enter x-coordinates in the x₁ column and y-coordinates in the y₁ column.
coaching_steps.hint: Make sure to match x₁ with y₁ values correctly. Each row is one point.
coaching_steps.why: Tables organize data so Desmos can perform regression analysis. The matching columns ensure correct pairing.

coaching_steps.title: Run linear regression using the tilde operator
coaching_steps.instruction: In a new expression line, type y₁ ~ m x₁ + b. Press Enter.
coaching_steps.hint: Always use ~ (tilde) not =. The tilde means 'find the best-fit parameters.'
coaching_steps.why: The regression finds the line that minimizes the sum of squared errors across all data points.

coaching_steps.title: Read the slope (m) and y-intercept (b) from the output
coaching_steps.instruction: Desmos displays m and b values in a gray box below your regression line.
coaching_steps.hint: m is always the coefficient of x. b is the constant term.
coaching_steps.why: These parameters define the unique line that best models your data.

coaching_steps.title: Write the equation in slope-intercept form
coaching_steps.instruction: Substitute m and b into y = mx + b.
coaching_steps.hint: Check the graph to ensure the line follows the data trend. Points should be close to or on the line.
coaching_steps.why: The equation allows you to make predictions and interpret the relationship.

coaching_steps.title: If asked for a prediction, evaluate at the given x-value
coaching_steps.instruction: Type f(x) = m x + b, then f(target_x) to get the predicted y-value.
coaching_steps.hint: For SAT, evaluate at the requested x-value to find the predicted value.
coaching_steps.why: The function model allows prediction of y-values for any x-value.

common_mistakes: Using = instead of ~ for regression. = defines a specific line; ~ finds the best fit.
Forgetting to match table headers correctly (using x₁ and y₁ but x and y in regression).
Using x instead of x₁ in the regression formula when using table columns.
Confusing slope (m) with y-intercept (b). m is the coefficient of x; b is the constant.
Not entering all data points into the table.
Using quadratic regression when linear regression is appropriate.
Overcomplicating with 2 points: with exactly 2 points, slope formula is faster.
Misinterpreting R²: R² close to 1 means good fit. R² close to 0 means poor fit.
Forgetting units in word problems: slope has units (y-units per x-unit).
Using regression when exact answer is required and points lie perfectly on a line (R² = 1).
confidence_hints: With exactly 2 points, the regression line passes exactly through both (R² = 1).
With 3+ points, the line is a best-fit approximation. Points will be close but not necessarily on the line.
R² indicates how well the line fits: 1.0 = perfect fit, > 0.9 = strong, > 0.7 = moderate, < 0.5 = weak.
A positive slope means the line goes up as x increases. A negative slope means it goes down.
The y-intercept is the value when x = 0. In many word problems, this is the 'initial value.'
Slope is the rate of change: 'for each 1-unit increase in x, y increases by m units.'
Check the graph visually: if the line doesn't follow the points, you may have the wrong model type.

sat_tips.two_points: If given exactly 2 points, use slope formula m = (y₂ - y₁)/(x₂ - x₁). This is faster than regression.
sat_tips.table_data: If given 3+ points in a table, use regression in Desmos. It finds the best-fit instantly.
sat_tips.scatterplot: If given a scatterplot, use regression to find the trend line equation.
sat_tips.interpretation: SAT often asks: 'What is the meaning of the slope?' Answer: 'The rate of change; for each unit increase in x, y increases by m units.'
sat_tips.prediction: SAT often asks: 'What is the predicted value when x = ?' Use f(x) = mx + b and evaluate.
sat_tips.negative_slope: Remember negative slopes represent decreasing relationships. Don't forget the negative sign.

examples_provided.question: A line passes through (2, 7) and (6, 15). What is the equation of the line?
examples_provided.explanation: With exactly 2 points, regression gives the exact line. m = 2 (rise/run = 8/4 = 2), b = 3 (y-intercept). R² = 1 indicates perfect fit.

examples_provided.question: Find the equation of the line that best fits: (1, 3), (2, 5), (3, 7), (4, 9).
examples_provided.explanation: The data is perfectly linear (y increases by 2 each time). R² = 1 confirms all points lie exactly on the line y = 2x + 1.

examples_provided.question: The table shows values: (0, 10), (2, 18), (4, 26), (6, 34). Find the slope and y-intercept of the linear model.
examples_provided.explanation: The data is perfectly linear. The y-intercept is 10 (value at x = 0). Slope is 4 (each unit increase in x gives 4 units increase in y).

examples_provided.question: A scatter plot shows points: (10, 22), (20, 34), (30, 48), (40, 62). Find the best-fit line and predict y when x = 50.
examples_provided.explanation: The data is nearly linear. R² ≈ 0.997 indicates an excellent fit. For the prediction, evaluate the model at x = 50.

examples_provided.question: A line has a slope of -3 and passes through (4, 12). What is its y-intercept?
examples_provided.explanation: The y-intercept is found by solving y = mx + b with m = -3, and the point (4, 12): 12 = -3(4) + b → 12 = -12 + b → b = 24.

examples_provided.question: Which of the following is the equation of the line that passes through (-2, 1) and (3, 11)? (A) y = 2x + 5 (B) y = 2x - 5 (C) y = -2x + 5 (D) y = 2x + 3
examples_provided.explanation: Calculate slope from two points: m = 2. Use point-slope to find b = 5. Option A matches. This is a classic SAT multiple-choice approach.

examples_provided.question: The scatter plot shows a strong positive correlation. The regression line is y = 2.5x + 100. What does the slope represent?
desmos_setup: Identify context: If x is years and y is population, slope = 2.5 people per year.

resolved_parameters.interpretation: For each 1-unit increase in x, y increases by 2.5 units.

examples_provided.final_solution: Slope represents the rate of change: for each additional year, the population increases by 2.5 people.
examples_provided.explanation: In context, slope is always 'the rate of change' or 'change in y per unit change in x.' This is a common SAT interpretation question.
